title: Combine CPU and GPU usage graphs into a single row
description: Reorganize the dashboard layout to place CPU Usage and GPU Usage graphs side-by-side in the top row, mirroring the current layout of CPU Power and GPU Power. This provides better visual comparison between related metrics.
notes: ORIGINAL USER REQUEST: Fifth, I notice that the Power graphs for CPU and GPU are combined; we should do the same for GPU Usage and CPU Usage, I think. They should be next to each other in the top row (where GPU Usage and CPU Usage are currently located).

title: Remove Token Rate graph from webpage
description: Remove the Token Rate graph from the llama-monitor webpage as it does not provide meaningful information - it doesn't change much and adds visual clutter without value. Keep the other performance graphs (CPU, GPU, Power) intact.
notes: ORIGINAL USER REQUEST: Second, the Token Rate graph is unnecessary. It doesn't change much.
close_reason: Removed Token Rate (60s) history graph from the webpage. The live Token Rate metric in the Server Status card remains as it displays the current value, not a history chart. All other performance graphs (GPU, CPU, Power) are intact.

title: Display slot progress and context remaining as horizontal bar graphs
description:
Add two new horizontal bar graphs at the top of the webpage, above the panels with live values:

1. Slot Progress: One bar per slot showing completion percentage (0-100%) based on 'next_token' stats from /slots endpoint
2. Context Remaining: One bar per slot showing remaining context before compaction

These require displaying values inline horizontally with the big green total cost value. May need Claude Code configuration settings to determine context limits.
notes: ORIGINAL USER REQUEST: Tenth, there are stats available from the llama-server which would be nice to have, but aren't currently presented. For example, what are the stats in 'next_token' on each slot (in the /slots endpoint) about? Can we display a horizontal bar graph across the top of the webpage, above the panels with live values and in-line horizontally with the big green value for total cost, with the number of bars equal to the number of slots, and the length of the bar is a percentage value from 0 to 100% for how complete that slot is on its task? How about another graph exactly like that, except the values are for the amount of context remaining before a compaction occurs? That relies on knowing Claude Code's configuration, though, so we can look at adding settings for that. DO NOT LOSE ANYTHING I'VE WRITTEN HERE, enter it all into the bead as I've written it, in addition to any other description you give.

title: Implement Today's Energy Usage with midnight reset and session continuation
description: Implement Today's Energy Usage tracking that resets at midnight each day. When llama-monitor restarts, it should pull today's energy value (if exists) from storage and continue accumulating, rather than starting from zero. This provides accurate daily energy tracking regardless of monitor restarts. The calculation method needs discussion to ensure accuracy.
notes: ORIGINAL USER REQUEST: Seventh, we need to talk about how the session energy is being handled, exactly. How is it calculated? Is it really reset only when the llama-monitor is restarted? Perhaps it should be a running calculation of Today's Energy Usage, reset at midnight, and if llama-monitor is restarted it pulls a value for today (if it exists) and adds to it, keeping track of how much energy has been used.

title: Calculate CPU usage from llama.cpp process only, not total OS CPU
description: Modify CPU usage calculation to track only the llama.cpp process instead of total system CPU usage. Current implementation uses totalized CPU usage from the OS which doesn't reflect actual model inference load. The calculation should consider only the cores that llama.cpp is configured to use (e.g., if configured for P-cores only with 8-16 threads, calculate usage based on those specific cores). Implementation discussion needed to determine exact calculation method.
notes:
ORIGINAL USER REQUEST: Fourth, if the graph for CPU Usage is using the totalized CPU usage from the OS, we want to not do that. We want to calculate our own CPU usage value based on how much the llama.cpp process is using, and only on the cores it is running on. For example, I have a CPU with 8 P cores and 8 E cores. The P cores are hyperthreaded. I have llama.cpp configured to only run on the P cores, and it will run with 8 threads most of the time but 16 threads for when it is doing batching. I'm not sure exactly how to calculate that, some discussion and back-and-forth questions using the grill-me skill might be needed.

ADDITIONAL: WMIC (Windows Management Instrumentation Command-line) may be able to help us get per-process CPU metrics. This could provide the process-specific CPU usage values we need without having to calculate from scratch.

title: Fix TUI mode - main.py --tui launches webserver instead of TUI
description: Fix the TUI mode in llama-monitor. Running 'main.py --tui' currently launches the webserver and displays webserver logging on the terminal instead of showing a proper TUI interface. The TUI mode needs to be implemented or fixed to actually display a terminal-based UI.
notes: ORIGINAL USER REQUEST: TUI mode does not seem to work. Running 'main.py --tui' simply results in it launching the webserver, whose logging shows up on the terminal instead of any kind of UI.

title: Add historical data viewer with user-definable timeframe
description: Add a historical data viewer to the llama-monitor webpage that allows users to view graph data beyond the current 60-second window. The database can hold data indefinitely based on configuration, but users currently have no way to access it without manual database queries. Implement a graph with user-definable timeframe (e.g., last hour, day, week, custom range).
notes: ORIGINAL USER REQUEST: First, it has no historical data viewer. The graphs only display the last 60 seconds of information, but the database can potentially hold data indefinitely, depending on how it is configured. The user should have a way to see that data without needing to go do manual queries against the database. A graph with a user-definable timeframe is fine to start with.

title: Fix timestamps to show human-readable hh:mm:ss format
description: Fix timestamp display on the llama-monitor webpage. Currently shows Unix epoch time which is not human-readable. Timestamps should display as hh:mm:ss (hours:minutes:seconds) without date or milliseconds, making it easier to read live values.
notes: ORIGINAL USER REQUEST: Sixth, the timestamps are broken. They show Unix epoch time, which isn't very human-readable. They should be simply hh:mm:ss, excluding the date and milliseconds.
close_reason: Fixed timestamps to show human-readable hh:mm:ss format by converting Unix epoch timestamps to formatted time strings in the web server's JavaScript code.

title: Change 'Session Cost' to 'Today's Energy Cost' with proper daily calculation
description: Rename and update the cost display from 'Session Cost' to 'Today's Energy Cost'. It should calculate energy usage for today only (using the Today's Energy Usage implementation) and apply the cost per kWh rate. This metric should remain highly visible as it's the most important indicator for users. Remove duplicate 'Session Energy' display.
notes: ORIGINAL USER REQUEST: Eighth, there should be a 'Today's Energy Cost' as well. Right now that's at the top as 'Session Cost' and there's a duplicate 'Session Energy' with the cost per kWh. I'm not sure this adds much value up there... then again, I do like that it is nice and visible, since this is the most important metric. But it should be 'Today's Energy Cost' and calculated in a very similar 'today' way as the 'Today's Energy Usage' as described in my seventh item.

title: Add settings page for configuration values
description: Create a settings page on the llama-monitor webpage to edit configuration values such as collection interval and other settings currently only editable in the config file. All relevant config file values should be presented on the webpage for easy editing. This addresses the missing settings page that was previously requested.
notes: ORIGINAL USER REQUEST: Eleventh, where is the settings page I requested? I see no place on the webpage to edit the configuration values, such as how often to gather the values. There should have been a bead describing it. Most values in the config file need to be presented on the webpage, we can discuss it though.

title: Set CPU and GPU usage graphs to always show 0-100% range
description: Fix CPU and GPU usage graphs to always display with a maximum value of 100%, regardless of actual peak usage observed. Currently, if peak usage was at 80%, the graph scales to show 100% as the maximum, making it difficult to interpret actual utilization levels. The graphs should always show 0-100% as a percentage scale.
notes: Fixed CPU and GPU usage graphs to always show 0-100% range by adding min: 0, max: 100 to chartOptions y-scale configuration in web_server.py. Graphs now scale correctly from 0-100% regardless of actual peak usage.

title: Add monthly cost graph with date-only display
description: Add a new graph showing cost data for the last month. The graph should display only dates in MM/dd/yyyy format without time information. This provides historical cost tracking for users to monitor usage patterns over time.
notes: ORIGINAL USER REQUEST: Ninth, we need a graph of the cost for the last month. This graph should display only the date MM/dd/yyyy, without displaying time.

title: Track both total PC power and llama-server process power usage
description: Implement power tracking for both the entire PC (CPU + GPU + System) and the specific llama-server process. The current implementation claims to track llama-server process power but has unverified effectiveness. Both metrics should be displayed to enable comparison and verification that the process-specific tracking is working correctly.
notes: ORIGINAL USER REQUEST: Twelfth, we should track both the power usage by the PC in total (CPU, GPU, and System), but also the power used just by our llama-server process. The latter is what it is *supposed* to be doing right now, but I have significant doubts that it is working as designed.

title: Add --verbose flag to control webserver logging verbosity
description: Implement a --verbose flag for the llama-monitor webserver. Currently, the logging is too verbose and displays excessive output. By default, logging should be minimal, with the --verbose flag enabling detailed logging for debugging purposes.
notes: ORIGINAL USER REQUEST: The logging for the webserver is too verbose. We should implement a --verbose flag so that most of it is hidden by default.

title: Update dashboard title from 'llama-monitor' to 'Llama Monitor'
description: Change the dashboard page title from 'llama-monitor Dashboard' to 'Llama Monitor Dashboard' to properly reflect the project name. The current title uses the project files folder name instead of the actual project name.
notes: ORIGINAL USER REQUEST: The title is currently 'llama-monitor Dashboard' - llama-monitor is the name of the project files, but the project itself is Llama Monitor. The dashboard title should reflect that. 'Llama Monitor Dashboard'

title: Display all values with 4 significant digits
description: Update the llama-monitor dashboard to display all numerical values with 4 significant digits. This ensures consistent precision across all metrics including CPU/GPU usage percentages, power readings, energy values, costs, and any other numerical data shown on the dashboard.
notes: ORIGINAL USER REQUEST: Values should be displayed with 4 significant digits.
